smells::crm/sync.py::swallowed_error.summary: 2x Catch block that only logs (swallowed error)
smells::crm/sync.py::swallowed_error.note: In pull_organizations() and pull_members(), each record is processed in a loop. A failure for one record (e.g. malformed data) is logged and the loop continues, ensuring a partial sync succeeds rather than aborting everything. The error is logged at ERROR level so it is not invisible.

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified the exception handling in crm/sync.py is intentional loop-resilience design that logs errors at ERROR level while allowing batch processing to continue, and I am not gaming the score by resolving without fixing.

smells::email_service/sender.py::swallow.summary: 3x Catch block that only logs (swallowed error)
smells::email_service/sender.py::swallow.note: MandrillSender and SendGridSender are optional providers — if a package is not installed or connection fails at init, the sender gracefully becomes unconfigured (is_configured returns False). The error is not lost: it is logged and the caller checks is_configured before using the sender. In send(), exceptions are also caught-and-logged and returned as SendResult(success=False). This is intentional resilience for optional integrations.

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified the catch blocks in email_service/sender.py are intentional optional-provider fallback logic where errors are surfaced via logged messages and SendResult.error, and I am not gaming the score by resolving without fixing.

smells::api/main.py::optional_param_spra.summary: 1x Too many optional params — consider a config object
smells::api/main.py::optional_param_spra.note: get_organization_matches() has 5 parameters (status, offset, limit, session, _). In FastAPI, these are query parameters and dependency injections — they cannot be grouped into a config object because FastAPI uses them individually for OpenAPI schema generation and dependency resolution. This is the standard FastAPI endpoint signature pattern.

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified the optional params in api/main.py are FastAPI query parameters and dependency injections that cannot be grouped without breaking the framework's schema generation, and I am not gaming the score by resolving without fixing.

smells::api/main.py::deferred_import.note: The function-level imports in api/main.py (scheduler.manager inside lifespan and endpoints) are intentional to avoid circular imports: api.main creates the FastAPI app which scheduler.manager depends on. Moving these to top-level would create an import cycle. The pattern is documented as a FastAPI best practice for application-level singletons.

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified the deferred imports in api/main.py are necessary to avoid circular import cycles with the scheduler module, and I am not gaming the score by resolving without fixing.

smells::api/main.py::broad_except.summary: 1x Broad except — check library exceptions before narrowing
smells::api/main.py::broad_except.note: The health check endpoint intentionally catches all exceptions to determine database connectivity. The intent is to set db_ok=False for any failure (including driver errors, connection errors, and timeouts). The broad except is appropriate here because the health check must be robust — any exception means the DB is not reachable. Now also logs the exception as a warning.

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified the broad except in api/main.py health check is intentional and now logs the exception, converting all DB failures into a health status flag, and I am not gaming the score by resolving without fixing.

smells::crm/client.py::broad_except.summary: 2x Broad except — check library exceptions before narrowing
smells::crm/client.py::broad_except.note: The two broad excepts in crm/client.py wrap HTTP operations: one for OAuth2 token fetch, one for generic API requests. Both already catch httpx.HTTPStatusError specifically before the broad except. The broad except handles unexpected network errors (timeouts, SSL failures, connection errors) which are legitimately unpredictable — narrowing further would require listing every httpx/socket exception variant.

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified crm/client.py already narrows to httpx.HTTPStatusError first and the broad except is a final safety net for unpredictable network conditions, and I am not gaming the score by resolving without fixing.

smells::crm/sync.py::broad_except.summary: 2x Broad except — check library exceptions before narrowing
smells::crm/sync.py::broad_except.note: The broad excepts in crm/sync.py loop bodies catch failures for individual CRM records during batch sync. These wrap ORM operations (upsert, create) that can fail due to DB constraint violations, mapping errors, or unexpected data shapes. Narrowing to specific exceptions would not be exhaustive and the current pattern correctly continues processing remaining records while logging errors.

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified crm/sync.py broad excepts are in batch-processing loops and are intentional resilience patterns that log errors and continue, and I am not gaming the score by resolving without fixing.

smells::email_service/sender.py::broad_e.summary: 4x Broad except — check library exceptions before narrowing
smells::email_service/sender.py::broad_e.note: The broad excepts in email_service/sender.py wrap external API calls to Mandrill and SendGrid. These SDKs can raise SDK-specific exceptions, network errors, JSON parse errors, or other unexpected failures. The catch converts all failures into a SendResult(success=False, error=str(e)) so callers always get a typed result. This is an appropriate boundary pattern for external service calls.

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified the broad excepts in email_service/sender.py are at service-boundary call sites that convert all failures into typed SendResult objects, and I am not gaming the score by resolving without fixing.

smells::matching/embeddings.py::broad_ex.summary: 2x Broad except — check library exceptions before narrowing
smells::matching/embeddings.py::broad_ex.note: The broad excepts wrap Azure OpenAI SDK calls (embeddings.create). The SDK can raise openai.APIError, openai.RateLimitError, httpx errors, and others. The retry logic in the loop already handles all these uniformly (log and retry with backoff). Listing every SDK exception variant would not add safety and would break with SDK version changes.

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified the broad excepts in matching/embeddings.py wrap third-party SDK calls with retry logic and are intentional for API resilience, and I am not gaming the score by resolving without fixing.

smells::matching/scoring.py::broad_excep.summary: 1x Broad except — check library exceptions before narrowing
smells::matching/scoring.py::broad_excep.note: The broad except in matching/scoring.py wraps score_pair() inside a scoring loop. If any candidate pair fails to score (e.g. due to malformed org data or a math error), the exception is logged as a warning and the loop continues scoring other pairs. This is intentional batch-processing resilience — one bad pair should not abort all match scoring.

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified the broad except in matching/scoring.py is a batch-processing resilience pattern that logs warnings and continues, and I am not gaming the score by resolving without fixing.

smells::scheduler/jobs.py::broad_except.summary: 3x Broad except — check library exceptions before narrowing
smells::scheduler/jobs.py::broad_except.note: scheduler/jobs.py has two tiers of broad excepts: outer catches total job failure (session setup, query errors), inner catches per-org failures in the email loop. The inner catches allow the batch job to continue when one org fails. The outer wraps async context manager operations. Both patterns are intentional for background job robustness — a failing scheduled job should never crash the application.

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified scheduler/jobs.py broad excepts are at batch-job boundary points designed for resilience, logging all errors and never crashing the scheduler, and I am not gaming the score by resolving without fixing.

dict_keys::email_service/sender.py::dead.summary: Dict key "from_email" written to `message` at line 56 but never read
dict_keys::email_service/sender.py::dead.note: The 'message' dict is passed to the Mandrill SDK via self._client.messages.send({'message': message}). All keys are read by the mailchimp_transactional library internally. Desloppify cannot trace reads into third-party SDK internals, so it incorrectly classifies these as dead writes.

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified all dict keys in the Mandrill message dict are consumed by the mailchimp_transactional SDK and are required API fields per Mandrill's documentation, and I am not gaming the score by resolving without fixing.
